vocabulary.definition: A line that shows numbers in order, used to graph solutions of inequalities
vocabulary.visual: A horizontal line with marks at 0, 1, 2, 3, ... showing where values fall

vocabulary.definition: A circle on a number line that shows a value is NOT included in the solution set
vocabulary.visual: x > 5: open circle at 5 means 5 itself is NOT a solution, but 5.1, 6, 7, ... are

vocabulary.definition: A filled-in circle on a number line that shows a value IS included in the solution set
vocabulary.visual: x ≥ 5: closed circle at 5 means 5 IS a solution, and so are 6, 7, 8, ...

vocabulary.definition: All values that make an inequality true
vocabulary.visual: For x > 3, the solution set includes 3.1, 4, 5, 10, 100 — any number greater than 3

launch.narrative: Detective Okafor is building a timeline. The suspect was seen at the scene no earlier than 2:00 PM, meaning the arrival time t satisfies t ≥ 2. She needs to plot this on a timeline to share with her team. How should she mark the number line?
launch.contextImage: Detective pointing at a large wall timeline with pushpins and string connecting events
noticePrompts: What does 'no earlier than 2:00 PM' mean about the time?
Which direction on the number line would you shade?
wonderPrompts: What would the graph look like if the suspect arrived before 2:00 PM?
How would you show the difference between t > 2 and t ≥ 2 on a number line?

explore.instructions: Graph each inequality on the number line. Decide whether to use an open or closed circle, then shade in the correct direction.
hints: Use an open circle for < and > (the boundary is NOT included). Use a closed circle for ≤ and ≥ (the boundary IS included).
Shade to the RIGHT for > or ≥ (values get larger). Shade to the LEFT for < or ≤ (values get smaller).
explore.label: Graph each inequality. Choose the correct circle and shade the correct direction.

discourse.prompt: How do you decide between an open circle and a closed circle when graphing?
discourse.sentenceFrame: I use a ___ circle when the symbol is ___ because the boundary value ___ part of the solution.

optional.stem: On a number line, x > 3 is shown with...
choices: An open circle at 3, shaded to the right
A closed circle at 3, shaded left
A closed circle at 3, shaded right
An open circle at 3, shaded left
optional.explanation: 'Greater than' uses an open circle and shades right (larger values).

optional.stem: Which inequality has a closed (filled) circle on its graph?
optional.explanation: 'Less than or equal to' includes the endpoint, shown by a closed circle.

optional.stem: A graph shows a closed circle at -2 shaded to the right. Which inequality is it?
optional.explanation: Closed circle means 'or equal to'; shaded right means greater: x ≥ -2.

optional.stem: Which value is a solution to x < 4?

approaching.explanation: x < 5 means 5 is NOT included (open circle) and values less than 5 are shaded (left).
x ≥ 3 means 3 IS included (closed circle) and values greater than or equal to 3 are shaded (right).
x > 8 means 8 is NOT included (open circle) and values greater than 8 are shaded to the right.
x ≤ 10 means 10 IS included (closed circle) and values less than or equal to 10 are shaded (left).

approaching.instructions: Sort each inequality by the type of circle it uses on a number line.
hints: < and > use OPEN circles because the boundary is not included.
≤ and ≥ use CLOSED circles because the boundary IS included.
approaching.label: Sort by circle type: open or closed.

onLevel.label: Match each inequality to its graph description

onLevel.instructions: Read the graph description and write the inequality it represents. Then graph it.

problems.label: Closed circle at 5, shaded right → write the inequality
Open circle at 3, shaded left → write the inequality

hints: Closed circle means ≤ or ≥. Shaded right means greater, so ≥.
Open circle means < or >. Shaded left means less, so <.

onLevel.stem: A number line shows a closed circle at 8 and is shaded to the left. Which inequality does this represent?
onLevel.explanation: Closed circle means the value is included (≤ or ≥). Shaded left means less than or equal to: x ≤ 8.

onLevel.label: Complete the table comparing how different symbols affect the graph.

extending.title: Find the Graphing Error
extending.correctWork: x > 6 uses a strict inequality (no 'equal to'), so the circle should be OPEN, not closed. Open circle at 6, shaded right.
hints: Look at the inequality symbol carefully. Is it > or ≥?
> means strictly greater than — 6 is NOT included, so the circle must be open.

extending.prompt: A theme park has two rides. Ride A requires height h > 48 inches. Ride B requires height h ≥ 48 inches. A child is exactly 48 inches tall. Which ride can they go on? Explain using number line graphs for both inequalities.
extending.sentenceFrame: For Ride A (h > 48), the graph has a ___ circle at 48. Since 48 > 48 is ___, the child ___ ride A. For Ride B (h ≥ 48), the graph has a ___ circle at 48. Since 48 ≥ 48 is ___, the child ___ ride B.

extending.instructions: Graph the compound constraint: the suspect's age a is at least 18 AND at most 35. Graph a ≥ 18 and a ≤ 35 on the same number line. What range of ages is possible?
hints: a ≥ 18: closed circle at 18, shade right. a ≤ 35: closed circle at 35, shade left.
The overlap is the solution: all ages from 18 to 35, inclusive.

connect.scenario: A detective determines that fewer than 12 people were at the scene. The inequality p < 12 represents the number of people p. She graphs this with an open circle at 12 and shading to the left.
connect.promptQuestion: Why did the detective use an open circle instead of a closed circle? What values of p are possible?
connect.prompt: The detective used an open circle because ___ means 12 is ___ included, so possible values are numbers ___ than 12.

exitTicket.stem: How would you graph the inequality x ≥ 9?
exitTicket.explanation: x ≥ 9 means 9 is included (closed circle) and values are 9 or greater (shaded right).
